key_insight: Time-resolved parameter estimation shows that a constant-parameter neo-Hookean Kelvin–Voigt model breaks down during inertial cavitation because the inferred hydrogel modulus and viscosity evolve over the event.
engineering_relevance: This gives engineers a way to diagnose when standard constitutive models are inadequate for soft materials under extreme loading, improving model selection for impact, cavitation, and high-strain-rate design.
method: Coupled laser-induced cavitation experiments with bubble-dynamics simulations for inertial microcavitation rheometry (IMR).
Estimated constitutive parameters over moving, overlapping windows using modified iterative ensemble Kalman smoother with multiple data assimilation (MIEnKS-MDA).
Tracked time-resolved shear modulus and viscosity within the NHKV model for PAAm and gelatin gels.
evidence_signal: Across hydrogel tests, the inferred shear modulus and viscosity generally decreased then plateaued, with weak temperature sensitivity in PAAm but pronounced temperature-dependent trends in gelatin and notable changes during the first two bubble collapses.
watch_item: Results are still constrained by the chosen NHKV model form, so apparent parameter evolution may reflect model mismatch as well as true material nonstationarity.
paper_title: Limits of constant-parameter constitutive models for hydrogels under inertial cavitation
